chunks.text: Hi. I'm going to slide close. Okay. All right. If I get in the picture as well, then I'm going to pull more in. Or go up to the other, but I think I might-

Welcome. Have you got enough space? Oops. I guess it'd be two chairs? Three chairs. There was two. All right. Well, maybe this way anyway. Thank you. Don't worry about the microphones. They'll pick up what you- Okay. All right. Well, good afternoon, and welcome. Trish, is it?

Yeah, good afternoon. Welcome. So you're obviously the Social Services and Community committee, and we're hearing submissions on the Disability Support Services Bill. Just a couple quick things I'll do, and it's for sort of everyone, not just for you. So I think probably a few people probably will watch this, but we're looking forward to hearing from everyone, and they can say whatever they want to.

But I think as a committee, we're really keen to hear from, if it helps people when they start thinking what does the committee want to hear. Really keen to hear about, firstly, what changes anyone would like to see in the bill. Second, what their biggest concern is. And third, what their solution would be.

I've also got a little timer, and we won't be completely strict on this, but we'll try and keep as best we can to. But different people we want to be heard. But just a little timer to hopefully make it just easier for people to go through. So just keep an eye on where they are. We've got five minutes or close to it for everyone. Might just quickly just go around the table and just get everyone to just briefly say who they are and what electorate or where they're from.

I'm Joseph Mooney. I'm from Central Otago/Southland, and represent what's called the Southland electorate. Kia ora. Priyanka Radhakrishnan, Labour list MP based in Auckland. Spokesperson for disability issues. Kia ora. Kahurangi Carter. Ngāti Maniapoto Tainui, and I live in Christchurch Central.

Kia ora koutou. Maria Menol Kapakingi, Te Tai Tokerau. Kia ora ra. Hi, Jamie Arbock. New Zealand First from Kaikoura, and also the King Country today with David Wilson. Kia ora. Hi, guys. My name is Laura McClure. I am with the ACT Party, and I'm based in Christchurch.

Nice. And I'm Maureen Pugh, the MP for West Coast-Tasman. And for vision-impaired, I've got short brown hair, wearing black with a purple jersey. Nice. Kia ora. I'm Dana Kirkpatrick, the MP for East Coast. I live in Gisborne and cover the big East Coast region.

So pleased to welcome you here today. Excellent. Well, with that, thank you very much. We now have five minutes with you, Trish. So fire away. The time is yours. Welcome. Just a bit of a heads up. I have a speech impairment. If you don't understand me, please ask me to speak again. But I do have a new message.

No, you're doing great. You're doing fine. Kia ora. My name is Trish McQuinn. I have cerebral palsy. I am 54. I'm ready to speak. I am here to talk about the Disability Support Services Bill. People with disabilities don't
want to be put away in a corner. We have rights and a life just as anybody else. I believe people with disabilities have the right to make their way their w-

I believe people with disabilities have a right to be heard, taken care, and not shoved into a corner. I believe people with disabilities have the right to make decisions that impact their lives. I oppose this bill because the SD has not been

inclusive of disability. This bill affects our lives. We as youth have been consulted from day one, and we have a voice. I believe as people with disabilities- I believe we have a right to be heard. I believe people should not be known what their lived disability. Because of

myself, I live by myself. I am now a year about waiting times in terms with those of a disability. For myself, I have the fact where I want to be. I was brought up in a home of compassion. So all my life, people have labeled me as a person who has not got a brain because of my speech. But look at me. I'm out in

this community, and I'm proud to be here. This is not where I trapped in the corner. I believe people with disabilities have a right to be heard. Now, the up my thing is that we have been put back in institutions, and that's what I don't believe in anymore.

We have a right to be doing what we want to do. I believe we have a voice. Well said. Very good. No, thank you very much, Trisha. No, no. I think we understood that clearly. Yeah. And you express yourself very well. So you're absolutely right.

You have a right to be heard, and thank you for coming and for giving us an opportunity to hear from you this afternoon. And yeah. You did better when you threw away the script. Most people- I'll hold the notes... most people do. Yeah. We want to hear from you.

Absolutely. And so you did very well. We heard clearly from you, and we really appreciate it. So thank you very much for coming and talking to us this afternoon. Well done. Did very well. Appreciate it. Have we got any questions? Any questions? Nope.

I think you left us speechless because you made- ... yourself so clear to us. But no, you did make your message very clear, Trisha. I really appreciate that, so thank you so much. Been awkward. You guys been there. Good challenge. And then I want to have a good look at my teeth.

All right. You got a stone? We need to bring a stone. No, I don't think we need to bring a stone. I think we have a stone table. Seeing all the doors, yeah. All right. Thanks, Trisha. Don't fires open, so we'll jump. Our next speaker. Good day, Nick. Good to see you.

It's a hard act to follow. It's yeah. No pressure. You have a challenge, Nick. You've got to-- Trisha's laid the weto on the line. I know. I feel the pressure. Kia ora koutou members. My name is Nick Rowin, and it's nice to see members again.

Mm-hmm. It's unfortunate that we have to make this a regular occurrence- Mm... coming to talk about how disabled people's rights are being assaulted on a regular basis. But for those of you who don't know, my name is Nick Rowin, and I'm a senior leader and a former member of Disabled Persons Assembly's National Executive Committee and a former chair of Wellington City Council's Accessibility Advisory Group. But I appear in front of you today in my personal capacity.

I want to be clear, I support the principle that disability support services deserve a durable legislative foundation. The absence of one has cost this country decades of fragmentation and inequality. The objective is sound, but this bill does not deliver it in any way, shape, or form. I've identified five structural failures serious enough to require substantial amendments before this bill proceeds any further.

Clause 8 inverts self-determination by forcing disabled people to exhaust their own resources, meager as they are, and that of their families, before the Crown accepts any obligation. That is not person-centered. That is beginning with the assumption that the person is a burden.

Second, this bill violates the New Zealand's obligations under the UN Convention on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities. The government's own regulatory impact statement acknowledges that consultation required under the convention did not occur before the bill was introduced to the House.

The government introduced legislation affecting 55,000 disabled New Zealanders while admitting it broke the rules. And this select committee process, while it's nice to meet all the members here today, does not make up for that breach. Third, natural justice. The Supreme Court unanimously held up the rights of family carers in the Fleming decision in December 2025.

This bill reverses that decision within months, before a single alternative support was in place. That is the definition of what natural justice exists to prevent Fourth, ministerial power. This bill lets a single minister design, alter, and potentially restrict disability support by written notice with no parliamentary oversight, no independent review, and most concerning, no floor of entitlement in primary legislation.

Good law should not work that way. And lastly, and fifth, families. 63% of carers of disabled children say they just do not have enough money to support their children or themselves. This bill responds to this situation by extending family financial obligations across aunts, uncles, cousins, and grandparents with no additional funding.

That is not a carer strategy. That is cost shifting onto people who are already at breaking point. I am here to ask this committee to do what the government would not do. Consult, amend, and put it right. The test is simple: Does this legislation leave disabled New Zealanders more secure or less secure?

With this bill as currently drafted, the answer is clear. I ask you to use the power that you have as members of this select committee to put things right. Thank you. I'm happy to take questions. Thanks. We have a question from Paula Enoki. Thanks, Nick. Quick question on the point that you make under ground three, which is that so much that is substantive in this bill is relegated to secondary legislation.

Do you have a view on what level of information you'd like to see in primary leg? I think it should be explicit in primary leg. If we're going to put the types of entitlements that disabled people and families receive in secondary leg, we're effectively hiding the entitlements in secondary leg, which is completely hidden from plain sight. We must place it into primary leg.

So would it be the types of entitlements, the criteria, and the amounts that you'd be wanting to see? Well, I'll draw a comparison. We do that with the Social Security Act. Mm. Everything is in plain primary legislation in the Social Security Act. I ask this committee, why are we making a distinction with this piece of legislation and hiding it away in secondary leg where no one can know what it is. No one can see any decision-making around what people's entitlements are, what the decision-making criteria is.

Why the distinction? Why the distinction for our most vulnerable members of the community? I might just ask you a last supplementary, Nicholas. Are you aware that currently there is no legislation really in this space, and things can be changed by a cabinet minute, by a letter from a minister. So this is actually providing clarity and transparency that's never been there before.

Exactly. So that's my point. There is no clarity. There is no legislative framework today. I'm not here to say that what's here today is right. What I'm here to say is put it right because it's not right now. And you have the opportunity to make some recommendations to put it right. And if this committee comes back with a recommendation to just give a free pass to the government on this piece of leg, then you're just effectively saying that all of the things that are wrong with this piece of legislation are just going to carry on, and we're going to

put families in poverty. We're going to put disabled people in a situation where they have no idea how their entitlements are going to be decided on. You have the power within this committee to make it right, to put it right. All right. Thanks for making a submission this afternoon, Nick. Tena koe. Thank you. Thank you.

Good afternoon, Helena. Hi. Welcome. Thank you very much. All right. So we've got five minutes with you, so- Okay. Thank you... the time is yours. I'm quite nervous, so this is my first- Oh, no. Go for it. Thank you for this opportunity to speak to you today, not as an advocate or a natural support, but as a mother of two adult sons who require full-time residential care. My name is Helena Faiti, although in this context, I am proudly Adrian and Don's mum.

I'm here to share why families like mine are worried and concerned by the Disability Support Services Bill, and why I believe it must be rejected in its current form. For years, parents like me have worked with schools, health providers, therapists, MSD, and other government agencies and their contracted providers to ensure our children are safe, respected, and supported in New Zealand as equal citizens.

This bill, in its detail, threatens that foundation in three ways. Firstly, it abdicates the state's responsibility. This bill states that families and whanau have responsibilities for the well-being of their members in the first instance. Nearly all the families I know operate this family-first principle and have done so from day zero.

Mm. Frankly, formalizing this principle is harmful. It deflects the Crown's duty onto the shoulders of exhausted and aging parents. My sons are now 32 and 38. They need 24/7 care and supervision, and I no longer have the energy to provide this safely in my home day in, day out.

By making state care sound like a contribution or a favor rather than a right, this legislation is signaling a silent withdrawal of government support For most New Zealand parents, legal responsibility for a child ends when that child turns 18. Our sons, as I said, are now 32 and 38, well beyond that age. Yet under this bill, families like ours would still be considered responsible in the first instance.

As the definition of family in this bill casts a very wide and interesting net, it tells us that under the first principle, public funded support is conditional upon utilizing available family and community resources. In families like ours, once my husband and I are no longer up to this role, this would mean that the responsibility lies with our brothers and sisters and uncles and aunts.

And if none of them are available, step-families, cousins, nieces, nephews will have to take up this role. This feels unsafe. Secondly, I am grateful that no one support changes for three years. But in 2019, this bill will give sweeping powers to the minister to set up new programs with minimal public or any parliamentary scrutiny.

It leaves the door wide open for the possible future income and asset testing without any baseline protections in law. Families who have planned and worked to secure a safe living environment for their loved ones are now living in fear that the rug will be pulled out from under them, and that they will be forced to cover impossible to predict costs out of their own pensions. Thirdly, I cannot ignore the context in which this bill was rushed to Parliament.

It was drafted and directly in response to the Supreme Court ruling that recognized some family carers as government employees. Instead of using that landmark decision to honor and fairly compensate families for the immense work they do, this legislation was designed to shield the Crown from further litigation and fiscal risk.

It prioritizes balancing the budget over the dignity and equality of our disabled loved ones. This makes me sad and worried. Love and necessity drive me and my husband to care for our sons, but love should never be a substitute for government infrastructure. The state has a moral obligation to provide sustainable long-term support for New Zealand's most vulnerable citizens. This bill is telling families like mine that our equality in modern society is some sort of fiscal problem to be managed and controlled.

Are we now somehow a burden or a second-class citizen? Disabled New Zealanders and their whanau deserve the legislative framework that protects their rights, not one that treats them as obstacles to avoid. I urge you to reject this bill. We must go back to the drawing board and build a system with the disability community, not a system done to them.

Well done. Thank you. Quinn? Thank you. Thanks. You mentioned that you already operate from, and many others already operate from a Families First issue. But that formalizing it is dangerous. Now, the minister has said previously that she's just codifying what already happens. Can you tease out-- I'm playing devil's advocate.

Yes. Can you tease out why codifying it in law is dangerous? So when you're a little bit younger you can do it. But when our children were little, I could afford not to work, and I just look at the young ones around me today that have their disabled children, and they are-- Sorry.

It's all right. They're having to work as well in this. And so if they have to work and do childcare and do that, but they still have to look at their own resources first, how are they ever going to be able to afford to own their own home or do things like that? I think if you say the family has to exhaust their resources first, that's just too much on humans.

Well, thank you, Hannah, for sharing your experience with us this afternoon. Appreciate it. Thank you. Well done. Thank you. All right. Next speaker is Assistive Technology Suppliers New Zealand, Gary Holmes, the CEO. Welcome, Gary. Thank you. ATS-NZ represents the businesses that manufacture, import, and deliver assistive technology across New Zealand.

Wheelchairs, communication devices, home modification, and mobility aids. We are the supply chain. We are not a disability advocacy group, and we want to be clear about that. Our concern with this bill is commercial and practical, and they come from our direct experience of what is happening when DSS funding settings change without notice.

So our biggest concern is clause 11 (3) and . These subclauses give a future minister explicit statutory authority to introduce means testing for DSS eligibility by written notice, without a parliamentary vote, and without any obligation to consult the sector. DSS has never been means tested.

If the government wants to change that, it is a significant enough decision to go through the parliamentary process. The second concern sits alongside it in clause eight, and it's already been referred to, the Families First principle. Where a family's resources and natural supports are assessed before equipment is prescribed, fewer people will receive funded assisted technology, not because the clinical need has gone away, but because the framework allows family circumstances to be weighed first. Our members see family situations up close.

Financial capacity on paper does not always mean a disabled family member's needs are prioritized in practice. When DSS funding settings can change by written notice, suppliers can't plan. We carry inventory, we employ specialist technicians. We make workforce and stock decisions 12 months or even more ahead. A sudden eligibility change gives us nothing to work with, and the practical result is reduced investment, withdrawal from harder to reach markets, particularly regional communities, and ultimately less competition,

which means higher procurement costs- For the funders this bill is designed to serve. The government has its own interest in a healthy supplier market. We're asking for four things, and none of them change the framework. One, consult the supply sector before ministerial programs are made with a minimum of 30-day notice period written into the bill. Two, require a parliamentary vote to introduce means testing. Remove or constrain Clause 11 (3) and .

Amend Clause 8 so that the families first principle cannot override a clinical AT prescription from an occupational therapist or a specialist assessor. And more time on this process. As a couple of people have already referred to, three weeks is really not enough. Mm. Just in closing, the government's own regulatory impact statement acknowledges that there was no consultation before this bill was introduced.

The supply side of the disability support system was not at the table. We're asking for the committee to recommend the safeguards that make this framework workable for suppliers and, more importantly, for the disabled New Zealanders who depend on the equipment that we deliver. Thank you, Mr. Chairman. Mm. All right. Thank you, Gary. I only have two quick questions for you.

One is, my understanding is there is a means testing mechanism already in place for DSS, and this is simply formalizing what that process so it's transparent. Are you aware of that? Well, I'm not fully aware of that. I'm actually relatively new to the industry. But I'm just referring to that particular point that I made about that, which is where I- Oh, look, if you don't know that- Yeah. Anyway... that's fine.

Yeah. The other question is, are you aware that this government has made the biggest investment ever into DSS services? $1.1 billion uplift last year and took 376 million this year, so a total of $2.933 billion. Right. The numbers are good, and the investment is always welcome. But if you want to get this legislation right- Mm... you need to get it right. And I think there's a lot of others that have spoken before me and a lot more you're going to hear from, who are giving you the practical realities- Mm... of some of the concerns that they see in this particular bill.

No one's denying that the government has spent what it has spent and invested in this particular sector, but you need to make sure we get the framework right. Thank you. Greg? Yeah. Just a quick question that I asked a previous submitter in terms of the level of information in primary versus secondary legislation. You've outlined some of it under point six, but do you have a view of how much should be in primary versus secondary leg?

That's probably a little too technical for my brain- It's okay... this afternoon. But, I think we're just putting the main concerns and the principles down. Yeah. That's up to you good folk to sort of determine how that is delivered. But the select committee process is a great one. It's great that you get the input from the end users- Mm... and from the suppliers in this particular situation.

I don't envy your job in trying to have to balance it all, but I hope that you take on board some of the concerns that have been raised today. No, thank you very much, Gary. Appreciate you coming. Thank you. Cheers. All right. Next submitter is Gary Williams. I think he's out of the way for you, Gary. Welcome, Gary.
Oh,

welcome, Gary. We've got the committee this afternoon, so we have five minutes or so with you this afternoon at the time. Awesome. We look forward to hearing from you. Yeah. Thank you for taking time out of your busy lives to listen to this today. Before I start, I want to recognize all the other submitters that have been speaking before me because this is really hard work that we have to do to get

here. Five minutes is not a long time for some of us to make our points. So I had a list of 50 points that I wanted to make. All important in my mind so that you can make the best decisions that you can. But I'm going to focus on one or two things.

It's half past 4 on a Tuesday afternoon. There's somebody out in the world who wants to get out of bed because their support person hasn't come today. Or their grandmother who's only had four or five hours sleep in the last week. And so the persona that you have
about disabled people in our community needs to consider that we don't fit into one neat, nice box. Um,

The concept of it is still oblivious to most of the 50,000 disabled people who qualify would get assist. And I know that we talk about disabled people, and there might be 900,000 of us, but the people that we're talking about now are those 50,000 disabled people

who get the most support from the system. And these are people whose lives are intertwined with other systems, like universities and daycare and housing and employment and education. So if I think about the idea of reassessing, I think that

the floor doesn't understand that disabled people that will be affected by this legislation because the disabled people affected by this legislation are the poorest of the poor. That means excluded from education, that means excluded from employment.

Some of us can't even leave our home. So I want to leave today with this, that you consult the people whose lives are going to be affected by this wide-ranging piece of legislation because I'm sure that you'll go home sometime. People can't leave their home. And

so please, please, please, don't chuck us out of the proverbial nest because you want to save a few million dollars because it's always got to be a false economy. I'd like to finish now because I get a bit emotional about this. Thank you for listening.

Well- Gary ... thank you very much, Gary. You expressed yourself very articulately and made yourself very clear. And thank you for making those points and coming. We've heard you loud and clear. Oh, my pleasure. Appreciate that. Gary's come all the way from Christchurch. Thank you. Come all the way from Christchurch. Well- Wow ... good on you. That's a- Yes... it's a long trip. Mm-hmm.
Been a long- Well, thank you, and hope you have a good day, good trip to Wellington as well. Appreciate it. The next submitter is the Cerebral Palsy Society of New Zealand. Daniel and Claire.

That's Daniel up there. Good afternoon. Welcome. Tena koe, nga mema o tenei committee. Ko Daniel Clay toku ingoa. Ko au te Hiamana o te ropu Hokai Nokorangi. Sure. My name's Daniel Clay. I'm the chair of the Cerebral Palsy Society. Cerebral palsy is the most common childhood physical disability and affects approximately 9,000 New Zealanders.

Many people with cerebral palsy have high and complex needs and need significant support. The vast majority rely on disability support services rather than ACC. The committee has asked submitters what their biggest concern is and what changes they want to see. On changes, I've tracks and changes to the infamous Clause 8- Yeah... and a few others, which I can hand around while I'm continuing with my statement.

And I'll get to that at the end. Our biggest concern with is that the bill assumes that families can provide more support if government support is reduced. The evidence shows otherwise. We surveyed 140 members for our submission who rely on those services. Almost half of the respondents reported providing more than 40 hours of unpaid care every week, and more than a quarter provide over 80 hours of unpaid care a week. Many whanau are effectively providing a second full-time job in unpaid care. They're already filling significant gaps in the

current system. And just on the question about codifying the current system or current practice, if that's the case, that's not right. The current system is not working and whanau are filling the gaps, and what this bill does is make it worse. Most respondents, unsurprisingly, told us that the current disability support services do not meet their disability needs. The consequences are substantial.

The vast majority of respondents reported impacts such as caregiver exhaustion, burnout, mental health impacts, relationship strain, lost employment opportunities, and financial pressure. These are not hypothetical future risks of a CBA. These are occurring now. More than two-thirds said they could not provide more support if funding were reduced, and that providing significantly more care themselves would simply be unmanageable.

Many respondents also expressed deep concern about the future. Parents are already providing substantial support well into their children's adulthood, and are worried about what will happen when they age, when age, illness, or death means they can no longer provide support. This highlights an important issue with the bill. There's a significant difference between the responsibility of parents raising a disabled child and the expectations placed on family and whanau members, widely defined, of disabled adults.

Those situations should not be treated as equivalent. Reducing support would also have wider serious economic consequences for individuals and the economy. Most respondents told us that reduced support would affect their household's ability to remain in paid employment. And over half said that means testing would have a major negative impact, with less than 10% anticipating no impact.

In other words, reducing support does not simply shift responsibility from government to families. It would also reduce workforce participation and increase financial pressures, driving more families and whanau of disabled people into economic hardship. We submitted that the bill should not proceed in its current form. However, if the committee wishes to proceed, we provided proposed amendments. These amendments, first and importantly, making clear that the government has primary responsibility for disability-related support

needs, while families play an important complementary role. Secondly, removing the provisions enabling means testing, and third, require meaningful consultation on future support programs. In summary, our message is simple. Families are already doing an extraordinary amount, more than they should. Many are at breaking point. They cannot do more.

The practical effect of the bill is therefore likely to be reduced support for disabled people, not increased family care. We ask the committee to recognize that supporting disabled people is not a matter of charity or family capacity. Mm. It is a fundamental responsibility of the government. Kia ora, and I welcome your questions.

Well, thank you, Dan. I might just kick it off. The minister has said on a number of occasions, including to our committee last week, that this bill will not change the entitlements of anyone at all. So you have that on one side, and then you have a government which has made a record investment, the biggest ever, into disability support services.

Why do you think this bill will do the opposite? Well, I'm not sure the minister said it won't change at all. I think what she said is it won't change anything straight away, immediately. Exactly. The purpose of this bill is to set up a framework, which I can agree with and the committee members didn't raise concerns with, a framework for future changes measured against the principles, including, I don't call it family first, I call it family substitution.

Family is always first. Mm. On your second point, so I anticipate changes. So if this is just codifying what's happened, two points. One, I don't think it is, and two, what's happening now is simply not acceptable. Your second point, sorry, remind me. Oh, just that the government's made a record investment, the biggest ever- Oh, yes. Yes... into disability support services.

Of course. Why do you think it won't- And we appreciate that. But for the individual disabled person and the individual whanau who are struggling now, providing 40, 80 hours of unpaid care a week of disability support, that means nothing. That doesn't help them. So we acknowledge that, we recognize and we appreciate that.

The fact is, the disability population is generally growing. Needs are higher, particularly in intellectual disability. There's a growing proportion of those. As I say, cerebral palsy, in the main, or for a lot of people, is high complex needs. And a lot of whanau, they can't get their whanau members to help.

These are expert, qualification required roles in terms of caregiving. So acknowledge that, but that doesn't solve individuals' problems. Oh, look, thank you very much, and thank you for making submissions this afternoon. Thank you for what you and your organization do for the disabled. Thank you, Dan. No, problem. Can I just acknowledge, too, the suggestions you've made for changing the bill.

Mm-hmm. It's really helpful to us. Yeah, appreciate it. As a former lawyer, I love that stuff. Nice. As a lawyer you're always the only one, isn't it? Yeah. Pretty much. It's true. Yeah. Exactly. All right. Next submitter is Disabilities Resource Center Trust via Zoom.

Show your name and voice. I wondered what that noise was. I know. Who's that? Oh, there we go. Now we can see. All right, Naomi, we can see you, and you look like microphone's off. Just double-check we can hear you. Tena koutou katoa. Oh, excellent. Yep. Loud. Excellent.

Um- All right. Welcome. Thank you. Nga mihi nui kia koutou. Thank you for the opportunity to speak today. My name is Naomi Freeman, and I'm the information services coordinator at the Disabilities Resource Center Trust in Whakatane. Our organization supports thousands of tangata whaikaha, people with disabilities, older people, carers, and whanau across the Bay of Plenty. Through our information service, community engagement and workshops, we hear directly from people affected by disability support services every day. We'd like to see the bill

strengthened to ensure that tangata whaikaha, people with disabilities and whanau remain at the center of decision-making. In particular, we'd like to see greater flexibility for funding to respond to individual circumstances, recognition of the vital role of family carers and whanau- Stronger safeguards around future changes, clear commitments that tāngata whaikaha, people with disabilities, will be meaningfully involved in decisions that affect their lives, and sufficient timeframes for submissions and engagement.

Our biggest concern is that the bill may create more barriers rather than removing them. Many of the people we support are already struggling with rising costs, limited respite options, transport challenges, and increasing pressures on whānau carers. We regularly hear from whānau who are exhausted.

Many people care around the clock and are concerned about what will happen if supports become harder to access or more restricted. We are particularly concerned that if flexibility is reduced, people will be forced into systems that do not reflect their individual needs. Disability is not a one size fits all, and social supports should not be either. Our solution is simple, trust tāngata whaikaha, people with disabilities and their whānau.

They know what supports are needed and how that support looks like for them. Create a system that is flexible and person-directed, and responsive. Ensure funding can be used in ways that genuinely meet individual needs. Family carers cannot pour from an empty cup.

Yet every day we hear from whānau who are exhausted, overwhelmed, and struggling to access suitable respite. We need urgent investment in respite options, particularly for those under 65. We cannot continue to rely on whānau carers to hold the system together without ensuring they have the support, breaks, and wellbeing they need themselves.

Make sure funding can adapt as people's circumstances change, rather than requiring people to continually prove their needs. We ask the committee to ensure this bill strengthens choice, flexibility, and dignity for tāngata whaikaha, people with disabilities, rather than creating additional barriers.

Whānau provide essential care that sustains tāngata whaikaha, people with disabilities, within their homes and communities, and significantly reduces pressure on residential services in the health system. The people we support want the same things as everybody else: to participate in their communities, maintain their wellbeing, support their whānau, and live good lives.

If there's one message we want the committee to remember, it is nothing about us without us. Policies may be written in offices, but their impact is felt in homes, communities, and whānau every day. Tāngata whaikaha, people with disabilities, must be partners in these decisions, not an afterthought.

Thank you for your time in considering our views. Oh, thank you, Naomi. Appreciate that. Quick question from me, and I've got a couple others. Our committee has heard that there's never been a sort of statutory framework for this before, and in fact, it's relying on things like 1990s cabinet minutes to do so. For the first time, let's make a transparent process, and it's reflecting what's current existing practice.

Just with your experience, can you just tell us, are you able to look into where these sort of policies or statutory mechanisms are currently for decisions that are made?
